content: Android is a Linux-based mobile operating system. Application code runs in a managed runtime and interacts with system services through framework APIs.
ART compiles and executes Android apps using ahead-of-time and just-in-time techniques. It handles memory management and garbage collection for app processes.
The Linux kernel provides process isolation, scheduling, networking, and device drivers. Android builds user-space services and security controls on top of that kernel layer.
Container-style isolation relies on namespaces and cgroups. Similar ideas appear in mobile sandboxes where applications run with restricted permissions and isolated storage.
Cross-compilation builds binaries for a target architecture different from the build host. Mobile projects commonly build ARM64 artifacts on x86_64 CI runners.
